what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard block that records a product/design decision to defer building an interactive UI-feedback/inspect mode with element-to-source-code resolution, and instead recommends a lightweight screenshot-plus-comment workflow.
observations: YAML frontmatter includes id item-8c7e2c43, board://item-8c7e2c43 address, type block, source claude_code, state current, title 'P8 · ❌ UI-feedback mode + inspect mode + element→ui://→code:// resolution (BD', author_session ch-3mpkjg3r, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-f9f4a6a6
Created and updated timestamps are identical, with a single history entry noting 'filed'
Body opens with a ❌ marker and references BD-A and BD-J
Describes a 'second mode' where Tim annotates live UI and the envelope auto-resolves to editable source files
Mentions existing partial pieces: data-ui-ref and code:// cards
Argues the lo-fi version (screenshot + comment) gives 90% of UI-feedback value for ~2% of the build
Cites Tim's hand-drawn red annotations on screenshots in MSG2 as evidence the simple workflow already communicates well
Ends with a 'faked-simply' directive: 'to give UI feedback, screenshot the screen and comment on it.'
standouts: Explicit 'faked-simply' workaround in place of building the feature
Strong cost/value framing: 90% value for ~2% build effort
Deferral of an 'architecturally elegant' capability based on observed user behavior
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated, and history
Uses board:// URI scheme for item addressing and part_of linking
Title includes a status emoji (❌) and a P8 prefix
Markdown body uses a level-3 heading and bold emphasis for decision rationale
Records a decision item in a noticeboard/ledger format
concerns: Title appears truncated: ends with '(BD' and missing closing parenthesis
Created and updated timestamps are identical with only a 'filed' history entry, so no record of review or approval
Assumes Tim's behavior and MSG2 context without evidence inside this file
'Faked-simply' directive lacks details on where the screenshot-comment workflow is documented or enforced
No explicit owner, review date, or criteria for revisiting the deferred element→code resolver
notes: A decision record arguing against building a live UI inspect/feedback mode now, proposing screenshot-plus-comment as the immediate substitute.
questions: What do BD-A and BD-J refer to?
What is MSG2 and who is Tim?
What exactly are data-ui-ref and code:// cards, and how complete are they?
Where is the screenshot-plus-comment workflow documented for users?
Is this decision approved, or is it a proposal awaiting review?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to record a decision about UI-feedback/inspect mode; the file does exactly that, so it matches.
apparent_themes: Lo-fi manual workaround over high-fidelity automation
Deferring elegant architecture until it is week-one necessary
intention_signals: It is trying to kill or postpone an expensive interactive UI-feedback feature by showing that a screenshot-and-comment process already satisfies the need, while preserving the element→code resolver idea for the future.
novelty: The explicit 'faked-simply' framing and the 90%-for-2% value argument are distinctive; it also treats a noticeboard item as a decision record that defers a feature rather than specifying it.
contribution: It contributes a documented rationale for deferring the UI-feedback/inspect mode, a concrete lightweight alternative workflow, a pointer to a parent item, and a clear statement that element→code resolution is a future capability rather than a current requirement.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard decision item that defers an interactive UI-feedback and inspect mode with element-to-source resolution in favor of a screenshot-plus-comment workflow.
intention_for_embedding: To record the rationale for not building live UI annotation and source-code resolution now, and to establish a manual workaround as the accepted short-term approach.
